data.content_ref: inline:The claim attributes collapse to a prolonged drought from c.1220–1160 BCE but excludes Egypt from the scope without explaining how Egypt—which depended on the same Nile hydrology and was subject to the same regional climatic shifts—survived diminished. The problem explicitly asks why Egypt survived while the others collapsed, yet the drought mechanism applies uniformly across the eastern Mediterranean. The account provides no Egypt-specific mitigating factor (e.g., Nile flood management, grain storage, or the river's buffering capacity against rainfall-dependent agriculture) to justify the scope exclusion, leaving the differential outcome unexplained.
